perturbation.original_text: ARC covenants and agrees that they will not, directly or indirectly, disclose or communicate to any person or entity any Confidential Information of the Company; except (i) in connection with the enforcement of ARC's rights under the Purchase Agreement, or (ii) as otherwise required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: ARC shall endeavor not to, directly or indirectly, disclose or communicate to any person or entity any Confidential Information of the Company; except (i) in connection with the enforcement of ARC's rights under the Purchase Agreement, or (ii) when disclosure seems necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'covenants and agrees that they will not' to 'shall endeavor not to' weakens the obligation to a mere attempt. Furthermore, replacing 'as otherwise required by law' with 'when disclosure seems necessary' introduces a subjective standard for disclosure, which undermines the clarity and enforceability of the non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.85(B)(1) outlines specific circumstances under which trade secrets can be disclosed, primarily under a protective order or with express consent. The modified text's vague allowance of disclosure 'when disclosure seems necessary' allows for subjective interpretation that undermines the protections offered by the Ohio Trade Secrets Act, which focuses on preventing unauthorized disclosure, not simply discouraging it.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1333.85 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1333.85 | Cancellation, failure to renew or substantial change in territory. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1333 Trade Practices Effective: November 9, 1994 Latest Legislation: Senate Bill 209 - 120th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF Except as provided in divisions (A) to (D) of this section, no manufacturer or distributor shall cancel or fail to renew a franchise or substantially change a sales area or territory without the prior consent of the other party for other than just cause and without at least sixty days' written notice to the other party setting forth the reasons for such cancellation, failure to renew, or substantial change. (A) Neither party shall be required to give to the other party such notice if any of the following events occur: (1) The filing of a petition in bankruptcy or an assignment for the benefit of creditors by the other party; (2) The filing of an involuntary petition in bankruptcy against either party, which petition is not dismissed within thirty days; (3) The cancellation, revocation, or suspension for more than thirty days of any permit required to be held by either party to authorize the handling of alcoholic beverages. The occurrence of any one of the foregoing events shall constitute just cause for cancellation or failure to renew a franchise or substantially changing a sales area or territory without the prior consent of the other party. (B) The occurrence of any of the following events shall not constitute just cause for cancellation of or failure to renew a franchise or substantially changing a sales area or territory without the prior consent of the other party: (1)
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information and introduces a subjective standard for disclosure, which undermines the original contract's intent and enforceability. The original text uses "covenants and agrees that they will not," creating a firm obligation. The changed text uses "shall endeavor not to," which weakens this obligation. Replacing 'as otherwise required by law' with 'when disclosure seems necessary' introduces a subjective standard, further weakening the obligation. The Law Explanation points out that Ohio Revised Code § 1333.85(B)(1) focuses on preventing unauthorized disclosure, but the new text allows for subjective interpretation, potentially undermining the protections offered by the Ohio Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, while not directly on point about trade secrets disclosure exceptions, speaks to the importance of clear agreements and just cause when altering contractual obligations, which is undermined by the introduced ambiguity. The changed text allows disclosure whenever it "seems necessary," creating a potential loophole that contradicts the original intent to protect confidential information and potentially conflicting with the spirit of trade secret laws focused on preventing unauthorized disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date hereof, ARC shall not, acting directly or indirectly, (i) have any ownership interest in any competing business, (ii) call on, solicit or have business communications with respect to a competing business with any of the Company's customers or prospective customers for the purpose of obtaining competing business from such customer or prospective customer other than for the benefit of the Company, or (iii) participate in the formation or start-up of a competing business.
perturbation.changed_text: For approximately five (5) years from the date hereof, ARC should generally avoid, acting directly or indirectly, (i) having a significant ownership interest in any potentially competing business, (ii) actively soliciting or initiating business communications related to a competing business with any of the Company's key customers or prospective customers, or (iii) playing a substantial role in the formation or start-up of a clearly competing business.
perturbation.explanation: The change introduces multiple ambiguities. 'Approximately five years' weakens the duration. 'Should generally avoid' makes the obligation non-mandatory. 'Significant ownership interest,' 'potentially competing business,' 'actively soliciting,' 'key customers,' 'substantial role,' and 'clearly competing business' are all vague terms open to interpretation, undermining the original clause's definiteness.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1331.02 pertains to contracts in restraint of trade. While Ohio law generally permits reasonable non-compete agreements, the changes here make the restrictions so vague that they would likely be deemed unenforceable because they lack the clarity and precision required to define the scope of prohibited activities, thus failing to provide ARC with reasonable notice of what conduct is actually restricted. The statute requires the agreements to be definite and reasonable, characteristics weakened by the modified language.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1331.02 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1331.02 | Prohibition against issuing or owning trust certificates or entering into combination, contract, or agreement. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1331 Monopolies Effective: October 1, 1976 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF No person shall issue or own trust certificates, and no person shall enter into a combination, contract, or agreement, the purpose and effect of which is to place the management or control of such combination, or the product or service thereof, in the hands of a trustee with the intent to limit or fix the price or lessen the production or sale of an article or service of commerce, use, or consumption, to prevent, restrict, or diminish the manufacture or output of such article or service, or refuse to buy from, sell to, or trade with any person because such person appears on a blacklist issued by, or is being boycotted by, any foreign corporate or governmental entity. Available Versions of this Section October 1, 1976 – House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly [ View October 1, 1976 Version ]
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the original non-compete agreement. The law explanation states that Ohio Revised Code § 1331.02 requires non-compete agreements to be definite and reasonable. The perturbation explanation highlights that terms like 'approximately five years,' 'should generally avoid,' 'significant ownership interest,' and 'potentially competing business' create vagueness. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'no person shall enter into a combination, contract, or agreement, the purpose and effect of which is to place the management or control of such combination... in the hands of a trustee.' Although the snippet focuses on trusts, the principle is that agreements should not be overly broad or vague, as the changed agreement is. The modifications undermine the clarity and enforceability of the non-compete clause, leading to a legal contradiction because it fails to provide reasonable notice of prohibited activities and is therefore likely unenforceable under Ohio law.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Closing (as defined in the Purchase Agreement) of the transactions contemplated by the Purchase Agreement, ARC agrees that all copies and originals of all documents and other materials containing or referring to the Company's Confidential Information as may be in ARC's possession, or over which ARC may have control, shall be delivered by ARC to Belcan immediately, with no request being required.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Closing of the transactions, ARC should attempt to return most copies and originals of key documents containing the Company's Confidential Information that are readily accessible, as soon as is practically feasible.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces several ambiguities. 'Should attempt' softens the mandatory obligation. 'Most copies,' 'key documents,' and 'readily accessible' are all vague qualifiers. 'As soon as is practically feasible' introduces a subjective and potentially lengthy timeframe, undermining the requirement for immediate delivery.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1333.85 concerns the misappropriation of trade secrets, which implicitly relies on the expectation that a company will take reasonable measures to secure its confidential information. By weakening the obligation to immediately return all confidential documents, the amended clause could be seen as a failure to adequately protect trade secrets. This ambiguity potentially undermines Belcan's ability to pursue legal recourse for trade secret misappropriation, as it would be more difficult to prove that ARC failed to take reasonable steps to prevent disclosure by not returning the information immediately.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the immediate return of all confidential information, while the changed text introduces ambiguities that weaken this obligation. The law explanation posits that Ohio Revised Code § 1333.85, concerning the misappropriation of trade secrets, implicitly relies on companies taking reasonable measures to secure confidential information. By softening the requirement to return 'all' copies and originals 'immediately' to 'most' copies of 'key' documents 'as soon as is practically feasible,' the changed text undermines the ability to protect trade secrets. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address trade secrets or the return of confidential information, the law explanation's argument, coupled with the perturbation explanation, suggests the modified text creates an ambiguity that could be interpreted as a failure to adequately protect confidential information, thus creating a contradiction by making the contract less enforceable under trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: ARC acknowledges and agrees that a breach of any of the covenants set forth in this Agreement will cause irreparable injury and damage to Belcan for which Belcan would have no adequate remedy at law, and ARC further agrees that in the event of said breach, Belcan, without having to prove damages, shall be entitled to an immediate restraining order and injunction to prevent such violation or continued violation, and to all costs and expenses, including Belcan's reasonable attorney fees, in addition to any other remedies to which Belcan may be entitled at law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: ARC acknowledges that a significant breach of the key covenants set forth in this Agreement could potentially cause injury and damage to Belcan for which Belcan may not have a fully adequate remedy at law, and ARC agrees that in the event of such a breach, Belcan may seek a restraining order and injunction to prevent such violation or continued violation, subject to proving significant damages, and may be entitled to costs and expenses, including attorney fees, if deemed reasonable by the court.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce considerable ambiguity and weaken Belcan's remedies. 'Significant breach' and 'key covenants' are vague qualifiers. 'Could potentially cause' reduces the certainty of injury. Removing the language 'without having to prove damages' and adding 'subject to proving significant damages' places a higher burden on Belcan. 'May be entitled' replaces 'shall be entitled' and makes the award of costs and attorney's fees discretionary. 'If deemed reasonable by the court' adds further subjectivity.
perturbation.law_explanation: Ohio Revised Code § 1331.11(B) allows for injunctive relief and the recovery of damages for violations of non-compete agreements and other agreements in restraint of trade. By weakening the acknowledgement of irreparable harm and introducing the requirement to prove 'significant damages', the modified clause contradicts the principle that injunctive relief can be granted based on the likelihood of future harm, even without a showing of specific monetary damages already incurred. The change makes it more difficult for Belcan to obtain a preliminary injunction, which is often crucial in protecting its business interests.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Section 1331.11 - Ohio Revised Code | Ohio Laws Skip to main content Back To Top Top The Legislative Service Commission staff updates the Revised Code on an ongoing basis, as it completes its act review of enacted legislation. Updates may be slower during some times of the year, depending on the volume of enacted legislation. Section 1331.11 | Jurisdiction of courts. Ohio Revised Code / Title 13 Commercial Transactions / Chapter 1331 Monopolies Effective: October 1, 1976 Latest Legislation: House Bill 1358 - 111th General Assembly PDF: Download Authenticated PDF Courts of common pleas are invested with jurisdiction to restrain and enjoin violators of sections 1331.01 to 1331.14 of the Revised Code. For a violation of such sections, the attorney general, or the prosecuting attorney of the proper county, shall institute proper proceedings in a court of competent jurisdiction in any county in which there is proper venue. When such suit is instituted by the attorney general in quo warranto, he may begin the same in the supreme court of the state, or the court of appeals of Franklin county. When such suit is instituted by the attorney general to restrain and enjoin a violation of sections 1331.01 to 1331.14 of the Revised Code, he may begin the same in the court of common pleas of Franklin county. Such proceedings to restrain and enjoin such violation shall be by way of complaint setting forth the case, and praying that such violation be enjoined or otherwise prohibited. Upon the filing of such complaint, and before final decree, the court may make such temporary restraining order or prohibition as is just in the premises. In any action or proceeding in quo warranto by the attorney general or a prosecuting attorney against a corporation, the court in which such action or proceeding is pending may, ancillary to such action or proceeding, restrain or enjoin the corporation and its officers and agents from continuing or committing during the pendency of the action the alleged act by reaso
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens Belcan's remedies, contradicting the principle that injunctive relief can be granted based on the likelihood of future harm, even without showing specific monetary damages. By weakening the acknowledgement of irreparable harm and introducing the requirement to prove 'significant damages', the modified clause contradicts the principle that injunctive relief can be granted based on the likelihood of future harm, even without a showing of specific monetary damages already incurred. The scraped snippet 1 states "Courts of common pleas are invested with jurisdiction to restrain and enjoin violators of sections 1331.01 to 1331.14 of the Revised Code...Upon the filing of such complaint, and before final decree, the court may make such temporary restraining order or prohibition as is just in the premises.". The modification requires proving 'significant damages' before Belcan can seek a restraining order, thereby undermining the ability to obtain injunctive relief based on potential future harm, as described in the Law Explanation.
